FT_IC1.evidence: Abstract states 'Data from the Adolescent Behavior Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study' and participants recruited across 21 US cities; explicitly ABCD data.

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured via 'self-report questionnaire' (abstract); specific smartphone/screen variable extracted as exposure.

FT_IC3.evidence: Mediation analysis with inferential statistics: 'Each additional hour of daily screen time at T1 was associated with a 0.12-point (95% CI, 0.04-0.20; P=.008) increase in Child Behavior Checklist depressive score at T2.' Screen time predicts depressive symptoms (non-SM outcome).

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric coefficients reported: β=0.12 (95% CI 0.04-0.20, p=.008) for screen time→depression association; mediation estimate 36.4% (95% CI 18.2%-63.6%).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: prospective/longitudinal mediation study with baseline (T1, ages 9-10) and follow-up (T2, ages 11-13) data; reports phone/SM→non-SM outcome.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (JAMA Pediatrics, 2025); no evidence of duplicate/preprint status.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is the INDEPENDENT variable (predictor of depression), not dependent variable. Paper reports screen time → depression association (non-SM outcome). FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: No PDF available; coding based on comprehensive abstract with full results and statistics.

FT_IC1.evidence: Abstract: 'Data were from the first three waves of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (5,596 9-10-year-old youth)'; explicitly uses U.S. ABCD Study data.

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures cyber victimization as specific online/social harm variable: 'experiences of peer (overt, relational, reputational) and cyber victimization' reported separately. Cyber victimization qualifies as online social behavior exposure.

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effects logistic regression with inferential statistics: 'SGM youth experienced elevated...cyber victimization (OR 2.35, 95% CI 1.77-3.11)' and 'Around 22%-28% of the disparities in current suicidal ideation or attempts were mediated by peer or cyber victimization.' Cyber victimization linked to suicidal outcomes (non-SM outcome).

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric values reported: OR 2.35 (95% CI 1.77-3.11) for cyber victimization; mediation 22%-28%; multiple ORs for suicidal outcomes (4.75, 5.87, 4.94 with CIs).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: longitudinal mediation study using first three waves of ABCD; reports cyber victimization as mediator of SGM identity → suicidal ideation/attempts.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry, 2025); no evidence of duplication.

FT_EC7_reason: Cyber victimization is a predictor/mediator of suicidal outcomes (non-SM outcome variable), not the dependent variable. Paper includes individual psychological predictor (SGM identity) and downstream outcome (suicidal ideation/attempts). FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: E6 applied: cyberbullying/cyber victimization measured separately from SGM identity predictor with reported coefficients. No PDF available; coding from detailed abstract.

FT_IC1.evidence: Methods: 'We implemented graph theory...in 3037 youths (ages 11-12 years) from the ABCD (Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development) Study.' Uses ABCD data explicitly.

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured as specific variable: 'greater somatomotor disconnection was associated with...longer screen times' (β=0.13, p=2.6×10⁻¹⁹). Screen time extracted as independent variable in brain-phenotype analyses.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regression with reported coefficients: 'screen time (β=0.13, p=2.6×10⁻¹⁹)' linking screen time exposure to somatomotor disconnection (neuroimaging outcome, non-SM outcome). Also reports sleep duration → brain architecture association (r=0.23, p<.001).

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric statistics reported: screen time β=0.13 (p=2.6×10⁻¹⁹); sleep-brain multivariate r=0.23 (p<.001); component regression coefficient β=0.15; associations with cognition (β=-0.06, p=4.8×10⁻⁵) and externalizing (β=0.05, p=5.0×10⁻⁴).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional analyses of year-2 ABCD data (n=3037) using multivariate predictive modeling, principal component regression, and linear regression with reported coefficients and p-values.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published archival report (Biological Psychiatry: Global Open Science, 2025); prior preprint noted in acknowledgments but this is the peer-reviewed published version.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is an INDEPENDENT variable predicting brain structure/organization and developmental outcomes (cognition, externalizing symptoms), not the dependent variable. Paper includes multiple individual psychological/behavioral predictors (cognition, psychopathology as outcomes) and cross-sectional analyses. FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: E4 applied: multivariate predictive modeling with reported coefficients and permutation p-values (not just feature importance/SHAP). Comprehensive empirical analysis with neuroimaging, context (SES, screen time), and behavioral phenotypes.
